problem_statement: As LLMs automate long-horizon vibe-coding tasks, a supervision gap emerges: non-expert users cannot precisely specify their intent or reliably verify complex AI-generated outputs, making them weak supervisors of strong executor models.
magnitude_evidence: "Quantitative research shows that collaborating with code agents unexpectedly increases the task-completion time for 19% (Becker et al., 2025)."
insufficiency_evidence: "existing methods lack a pre-execution interaction layer ... They fail to help users elicit and structure their intent before the model acts"; AI critique and debate "largely rely on post-hoc evaluation. This is inefficient for long-horizon generation"
solution_mechanisms: recursive intent decomposition into a requirement tree
closed-form questions to reduce cognitive load
adaptive DontCare/DontKnow handling and clarification cycles
RL optimization from online weak-supervisor feedback
user reward + progressive reward + outcome reward
notes: Table/figure regions are OCR-garbled (Tables 1-3, Figures 2-9) but body text and key claims are legible; requirement-level oversight only, code-level supervision left to future work.
